input.title: String Concatenation in Loop

output.technical: Strings are immutable in Python. Using `+=` in a loop creates a new string object and copies the old contents every iteration, leading to O(N^2) complexity.
output.beginner: Because words cannot be changed in Python, adding a letter forces Python to make a complete copy of the whole word every single time.
output.analogy: Like painting a new identical portrait every time you want to add a freckle.
output.solution: Collect parts in a list and join them at the end using `"".join(list)`.
output.prevention: Always use `.join()` when combining many string fragments.
output.optimized_code:
parts = []
for i in range(10000):
    parts.append(str(i))
count_0 = "".join(parts)

input.title: String Concatenation in Loop

output.technical: Strings are immutable in Python. Using `+=` in a loop creates a new string object and copies the old contents every iteration, leading to O(N^2) complexity.
output.beginner: Because words cannot be changed in Python, adding a letter forces Python to make a complete copy of the whole word every single time.
output.analogy: Like painting a new identical portrait every time you want to add a freckle.
output.solution: Collect parts in a list and join them at the end using `"".join(list)`.
output.prevention: Always use `.join()` when combining many string fragments.
output.optimized_code:
parts = []
for i in range(10000):
    parts.append(str(i))
count_40 = "".join(parts)
